source_metadata.title_raw: Sanford Kahn - A-99-296 - December 21, 1999 - Long Beach

content.full_text:
File No. A-99-296 
Page3
FAIR  POLITICAL  PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 958 I 2-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886
December 21, 1999
This advice letter is SUPERSEDED to the extent it conflicts with Section 89501(b)(1) and 
Regulation 18932.1, which establish that the exception for earned income from the honorarium 
prohibition does not apply where the sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, or 
profession is making speeches. (See the earned income exception analysis in the Halderman 
Advice Letter, No. I-08-073.)
Sanford Kahn
3041 E. 3rd Street #2
Long Beach, California 90814
Re: Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-99-296
Dear Mr. Kahn:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the honoraria provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please bear in mind that this letter is based on the facts 
you have presented to us. The Commission does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In 
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
I. QUESTIONS
You are a candidate for the Assembly seat in the 54th Assembly District and have inquired 
as to the following:
1. May you receive compensation for a presentation you will give on March 16, 2000, to 
the Painting and Decorating Contractors of America?
2. May you schedule additional seminars for the Summer and Fall of 2000?
II. CONCLUSION
1 Government C de sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-296 
Page4 
You may receive compensation for presentations you will give as a seminar leader 
provided that you can meet regulatory requirements for recordkeeping consistent with the 
operation of a "bona fide" business. Such compensation will be characterized and reportable as 
earned income, and is not a prohibited honorarium, so long as your seminar activities are a 
continuation of a bona fide business in which you have been engaged for many years prior to 
your candidacy for elective state office.
Under these circumstances, you may accept compensation for a presentation scheduled 
March 16, 2000, in addition to compensation for seminars scheduled for the Summer and Fall of 
2000. Accordingly, you may schedule these seminars without being in violation of the Act.
Ill. FACTS
You are running for nomination in the March 7, 2000, primary election for the California 
Assembly seat in the 54th Assembly District. You are not an incumbent.
You have a business as a seminar leader giving economic/business presentations to 
national trade and professional associations at their meetings. Your goal is to give members of 
those associations an understanding of current and future business trends and the impact that 
these trends will have on their planning and profits. The seminar is part lecture and part question 
and answer session, with the attendees participating. For these services, you are paid a fee as a 
seminar leader.
Earlier this year, you were hired to give a presentation to the Painting and Decorating 
Contractors of America in Tampa, Florida, on March 16, 2000. You are currently working on 
scheduling a few more seminars for the Summer and early Fall of 2000.
In our telephone call, you stated that the entire amount of the income to your business is 
compensation paid to you in exchange for your seminar presentations. You also said that you 
maintain business records for tax reporting purposes and file income tax returns declaring the 
income from your business, a primary source of support. Additionally, you indicated that you 
have been a seminar leader for approximately 20 years and that your clients seek your services 
because you offer specialized expertise in evaluating business trends.
IV. ANALYSIS
The Act declares that public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Additionally, a 
primary purpose of the Act is to ensure that receipts and expenditures in election campaigns be 
fully and truthfully disclosed so that voters may be fully informed and improper practices may be 
prohibited. (Section 81002(a).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 89502(b) provides, in part, 
that no candidate for elective state office shall accept any honorarium. "Honorarium" is defined 
as any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at 
any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. 


File No. A-99-296 
Page5 
(Section 89501(a).) "Speech given" as used in Section 89501, means a public address, 
oration, or other form of oral presentation, and includes participation in a panel, seminar, or 
debate. (Regulation 18931.1.)
Since you are a candidate for the State Assembly2 who is running in the March 7, 2000, 
primary election, the prohibition against receiving honoraria applies to you. To comply with the 
Act, you may not receive compensation in any form for a speech given for any purpose, unless 
the activity falls within a specific statutory or regulatory exception to the definition of 
''honorarium."
Despite this prohibition, Section 89501(b) provides an exception which may be 
applicable to your situation. This section states that the term "honorarium" does not include:
"Earned income for personal services which are customarily 
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, 
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, 
insurance, real estate, banking, or building contracting, unless the 
sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, or profession is 
making speeches." (Section 89501(b)(l).)
Regulation 18932.1 further explains that a business, in existence for two or more years, is 
presumed to be "bona fide" when:
"...the owner of the business has maintained the following 
documents for the two calendar years immediately preceding the 
year in which the consideration for the payment was provided:
(1) Books and records of accounting consistent with the
operation of a business; and
(2) Copies of tax returns filed in connection with the operation of
the claimed business." (Regulation 18932.l(a)(l) and (a)(2).)
You stated that your business has been in existence for approximately 20 years and that 
you maintain business records and file income tax returns declaring the income earned from your 
business. Therefore, it appears that you do meet the requirements cited above and that your
2 "Elective state office" means the office of Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Attorney General, Insurance 
Commissioner, Controller, Secretary of State, Treasurer, Superintendent of Public Instruction, Member of the 
Legislature, member elect d to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement System, and 
member of the State Board of Equalization. (Section 82024.)


File No. A-99-296 
Page6 
business as a seminar leader will be classified as a "bona fide business." However, Regulation 
18932.1(d) provides an explicit restriction which states that a business whose "predominant 
activity" is making speeches is not ''bona fide" for purposes of Section 89501.
According to Regulation 18932.3, "speechmaking" is presumed to be the "predominant 
activity" of an individual's business, trade, or profession in existence for one year or more if:
"(l) During the 12-month period prior to and including the date 
of the speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on an 
individual's business, trade, or profession is devoted to the 
preparation and/or delivery of speeches; or
(2) During the 12-month period prior to and including the date of 
the speech more than 50 percent of the gross income of the 
individual's business, trade, or profession is derived from the 
preparation and/or delivery of speeches." (Regulation 18932.3.)
As a result, the restriction given at Regulation 18932.1(d) seems to mean that your 
business does not qualify for the "bona fide" business exception since your entire business 
income is paid to you in exchange for your presentations, generally considered to be speeches. 
However, while the definition of "speech" in Regulation 18931.1 is broad, not all public 
speaking is governed by that definition. (Tribe Advice Letter, No. A-95-310.) In the Leach 
Advice Letter, No. A-97-160 (enclosed), we determined that earned income is based on the 
market value of services rendered and, therefore, differs from an honorarium which reflects 
values associated with the status of the speaker. Specifically, we have recognized that 
compensation in exchange for instructional presentations and training activities offered through a 
bona fide business and in an area of one's established expertise is earned income. (Ricci Advice 
Letter, No. A-97-225.) Consequently, evaluating the character of the compensation paid to you 
as a seminar leader is more determinative of whether you may receive such payments without 
violating the honorarium ban.
You have indicated that you have been in business for approximately 20 years and that 
you advertise your services as a seminar leader to various trade and professional associations 
which elect either to hire or not to hire you based on the specialized expertise you offer. 
Consequently, any payments received by you as a seminar leader presumably reflect the value of 
the presentations for which you were hired and cannot be attributed to your status as a candidate 
for the Assembly. As we concluded in both the Tribe and the Leach Advice Letters, payments in 
such cases are properly characterized as income earned as compensation for services of 
commensurate value, not for a "speech" within the meaning of Regulation 18931.1. 
Furthermore, in the Leach Advice Letter, we applied this analysis to an incumbent, concluding 
that the elected official could accept compensation in return for instructional presentations 
offered through her bona fide business as long as the regulatory recordkeeping requirements 
were met. However, we determined that the elected official could receive compensation only for


File No. A-99-296 
Page7 
presentations on subjects in which she had offered compensated instruction prior to election and 
before becoming subject to provisions of the Act.
Under these circumstances, your longstanding business is ''bona fide" within the meaning 
of Sections 89501 through 89506 if you meet the record keeping criteria of Regulation 18932.1. 
If you are able to furnish the required documentation, you may continue to give instructional 
presentations before groups of clients on subjects you taught before you became a candidate for 
elective state office. Therefore, you may receive compensation for a presentation you will give 
on March 16, 2000, to the Painting and Decorating Contractors of America. You also may 
schedule and receive compensation for additional seminars for the Summer and Fall of 2000. 
Your customary compensation for such presentations will be regarded and reportable as earned 
income and not as honoraria.
Please note that although you may accept compensation for subjects on which you have 
customarily offered presentations, the honorarium ban still applies to you for any public speaking 
on other subjects. Additionally, as you may conclude from our analysis, you are prohibited from 
receiving any honorarium for speeches you may be asked to give due to your status as a 
candidate or as a public official, whatever the subject may be. (Section 89502.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
By: Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:NB:klw 
Enclosure
